### Normalizing race time and sector time

**Race time normalization.** Raw finishing times are not directly comparable, because runners arrive with different pre-race ability and each race year has its own conditions. A 35-hour UTMB can mean very different things for two runners with very different UTMB Index values. To handle this, I estimate each runner's expected race time from their pre-race ability and race year. The difference between expected and actual race time becomes the normalized race outcome. If a runner finishes faster than expected, their normalized performance is positive; if they finish slower than expected, it is negative. This gives us a way to ask who performed well **relative to expectation**, not only who was fastest overall.

**Sector time normalization.** For pacing, raw sector times have the same problem, but even more strongly. A sector time depends on runner ability, race year, sector distance, elevation, and terrain. Instead of normalizing raw sector times directly, I normalize **sector time shares**: the fraction of a runner's total moving time spent in each sector. This keeps the focus on the shape of the race. For example, did a runner spend more of their total race time than expected in the early climbs, or less than expected late in the race? The expected sector share is estimated from runner ability, year, and sector. Comparing actual share with expected share gives a sector pacing score: positive values mean the runner moved through that sector faster than expected relative to their own race, while negative values mean they spent more time there than expected.
